name: Robert "Bob" Henriksen
chief_complaint: I've been getting more and more winded over the past couple weeks, and I feel a heaviness in my chest on the right side
onset_description: Progressive worsening dyspnea over 2-3 weeks. Initially noticed only with exertion (climbing stairs), now present with minimal activity. Dull right-sided chest heaviness developed about 10 days ago. Has lost 8 pounds unintentionally over the past month. Known history of non-small cell lung cancer diagnosed 8 months ago, currently on chemotherapy.
setting: Oncology clinic, referred to ED for evaluation

progressive_dyspnea.description: I just can't do what I used to. Walking from the car to the clinic had me winded. Two weeks ago I could at least go up a flight of stairs.
progressive_dyspnea.character: exertional at first, now present at rest with minimal activity

right_sided_chest_heaviness.description: It's not sharp pain, more like a weight sitting on the right side of my chest. Feels full and uncomfortable.
right_sided_chest_heaviness.location: right lower chest and right lateral chest

dry_cough.description: I've had this nagging cough that won't quit. Nothing comes up, it's just dry and annoying.

fatigue.description: I'm just worn out. More than my usual chemo tiredness. I sleep 10-12 hours and still feel drained.
fatigue.character: profound, persistent, not relieved by rest
alleviating_factors: rest helps somewhat but doesn't resolve it

decreased_appetite.description: Food just doesn't appeal to me. My wife makes my favorite meals and I can only eat a few bites. I've lost about 8 pounds.
decreased_appetite.character: progressive anorexia with unintentional weight loss

orthopnea.description: I can't lie flat anymore. Started needing two pillows a couple weeks ago, now I'm up to three or I feel like I'm suffocating.
orthopnea.character: dyspnea when supine, relieved by upright positioning

labs.CBC: WBC 3.8 (low — chemotherapy-related leukopenia), Hgb 10.2 (low — anemia), Plt 142 (low-normal)
labs.BMP: Na 133 (mildly low), K 4.3, Cr 1.2, Glucose 94, BUN 22
labs.chest_xray: Large right-sided pleural effusion with meniscus sign, occupying approximately lower one-third of right hemithorax. Mediastinal structures midline. Known 3.2cm right upper lobe mass unchanged. Left lung clear.
labs.CT_chest: Large right-sided pleural effusion, estimated 1.5L. Right upper lobe mass 3.2cm with mediastinal lymphadenopathy. No PE. Compressive atelectasis of right lower lobe.
labs.pleural_fluid_analysis: Exudative effusion (meets Light's criteria). LDH 245, protein 4.1, glucose 52 (low), pH 7.28. Cytology pending. WBC 2800 with lymphocyte predominance.
labs.ABG: pH 7.44, pCO2 34 mmHg, pO2 72 mmHg (mild hypoxemia)

past_medical_history: Non-small cell lung cancer (adenocarcinoma), Stage IIIB, diagnosed 8 months ago — currently on carboplatin/pemetrexed cycle 4 of 6
surgical_history: Mediastinoscopy with biopsy (8 months ago, diagnostic)
Port-a-cath placement for chemotherapy (7 months ago)
medications: Carboplatin/pemetrexed chemotherapy (most recent cycle 10 days ago)
Dexamethasone 4mg on chemo days and day after
Vitamin B12 1000mcg injection every 9 weeks (pemetrexed requirement)
allergies: Contrast dye (mild hives — premedicated with diphenhydramine and prednisone for CT)

social_history.smoking: Smoked 1 pack/day for 35 years, quit 10 years ago (35 pack-year history)
social_history.alcohol: Rare, maybe a beer on holidays. Used to drink more socially.
social_history.occupation: Retired high school history teacher and football coach. Coached for 32 years.
social_history.living_situation: Lives with his wife of 45 years in their family home. Two adult children live nearby and visit regularly.
social_history.exercise: Used to walk 2 miles daily, now can barely make it around the house
social_history.diet: Poor appetite currently. Wife prepares meals. Has lost 18 lbs total since diagnosis.

conditions.father: Colon cancer at age 72, survived. Died at 85 from heart failure.
conditions.mother: Breast cancer at age 65, survived. Died at 82 from dementia.

personality: stoic, dignified, and quietly resilient. Doesn't complain much and may understate symptoms to avoid worrying his wife. Has a calm, steady demeanor from decades of coaching. Occasionally shows dry humor. Has come to terms with his diagnosis but is not ready to stop fighting.
communication_style: Measured and thoughtful. Speaks in complete sentences with a calm, steady voice. Defers to his wife for exact dates and medication names ('Helen, when did I start that new pill?'). Will answer all questions honestly but won't volunteer information about how scared he is. May reference his coaching career when describing his mindset ('I've always told my boys to stay tough').
pain_description_style: Understates discomfort consistently. Says 'it's not really pain, just heaviness' and 'I've had worse.' Will rate things lower than they probably are. Describes symptoms in functional terms ('I used to be able to mow the lawn, now I can't walk to the mailbox'). His wife may interject that things are worse than he's letting on.

critical_findings: Large malignant pleural effusion (right-sided, ~1.5L) in setting of Stage IIIB NSCLC
Exudative effusion meeting Light's criteria with low glucose and low pH (poor prognostic indicators)
Progressive dyspnea and orthopnea indicating functional impairment
Compressive atelectasis of right lower lobe
Significant weight loss (18 lbs since diagnosis, 8 lbs in past month)
Hypoalbuminemia and hyponatremia reflecting malnutrition and malignancy
Morphine allergy — important for pain management planning
Contrast dye allergy — requires premedication for imaging
rubric.diagnosis: Malignant Pleural Effusion secondary to Stage IIIB Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer
recommended_interventions: Therapeutic thoracentesis (large-volume, up to 1-1.5L with monitoring for re-expansion pulmonary edema)
Send pleural fluid for cytology, cell count, LDH, protein, glucose, pH, cultures, and tumor markers
Continuous pulse oximetry during and after thoracentesis
Post-procedure chest X-ray to confirm lung re-expansion and rule out pneumothorax
Oncology consultation regarding disease progression and treatment plan modification
Discuss indwelling pleural catheter (PleurX) or pleurodesis if effusion recurs
Nutritional support — dietitian consult for cancer cachexia
Pain management avoiding morphine (use hydromorphone or fentanyl if opioid needed)
Transfusion evaluation given Hgb 10.2 and symptomatic anemia
Psychosocial support and palliative care consult for quality of life optimization
Updated staging discussion with oncology team
